source_paper_title: A Class of Dissimilarity Semimetrics for Preference Relations
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Nishimura_2024, title={A Class of Dissimilarity Semimetrics for Preference Relations}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0351}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0351}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Nishimura, Hiroki and Ok, Efe A.}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2249–2270} }
context:
Section 4, page 25 (end of Section 4, after Table 1)

In passing, we note that as an immediate consequence of Theorem 4.1, we have \(\mathrm{diam}_D(A(X)) \ge \mathrm{diam}_D(P(X)) \ge n2^{n-1} + 2 - 2^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} - 2^{\lceil n/2 \rceil}.\) We do not presently know whether or not either of these inequalities hold as equalities.
title: Determine diameter of acyclic and preorder preference spaces under top-difference semimetric
background_latex:
Let \(X\) be a finite set of alternatives with \(|X|=n\ge 2\). An (acyclic) preference relation in the paper is modeled as an \emph{acyclic order} \(\%\) on \(X\), i.e., a reflexive binary relation whose strict (asymmetric) part \(\succ\) contains no directed cycle: there do not exist pairwise distinct \(z_1,\dots,z_k\in X\) with \(z_1\succ z_2\succ\cdots\succ z_k\succ z_1\).

For any binary relation \(R\) on \(X\) and any nonempty subset \(S\subseteq X\), the set of \(R\)-maximal elements of \(S\) is
\[M(S,R):=\{x\in S: \text{there is no }y\in S\text{ with }y\,R^{>}\,x\},\]
where \(R^{>}\) denotes the asymmetric part of \(R\). For acyclic orders \(\%\), every nonempty finite \(S\) has \(M(S,\%)\neq\varnothing\).

Define the \emph{top-difference semimetric} \(D\) on the set \(A(X)\) of all acyclic orders on \(X\) by
\[D(\%,\mathcal{D}) := \sum_{S\subseteq X} \bigl| M(S,\%)\,\triangle\, M(S,\mathcal{D})\bigr|,\]
where \(\triangle\) is symmetric difference and \(|\cdot|\) is cardinality. (Singleton and empty \(S\) contribute \(0\), but the definition sums over all \(S\subseteq X\).)

For any subset \(\mathcal{C}\subseteq A(X)\), its diameter with respect to \(D\) is
\[\operatorname{diam}_D(\mathcal{C}) := \max\{ D(\%,\mathcal{D}) : \%,\mathcal{D}\in \mathcal{C}\},\]
which is well-defined since \(\mathcal{C}\) is finite when \(X\) is finite.

Let \(P(X)\subseteq A(X)\) denote the set of all preorders on \(X\) (reflexive and transitive relations).
problem_definition_latex:
Compute (or give an exact closed-form expression for)
\[\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\quad\text{and/or}\quad \operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\]
as functions of \(n=|X|\), where \(D\) is the top-difference semimetric
\[D(\%,\mathcal{D}) := \sum_{S\subseteq X} \bigl| M(S,\%)\,\triangle\, M(S,\mathcal{D})\bigr|\]
on acyclic orders \(\%,\mathcal{D}\in A(X)\), and \(P(X)\) denotes the class of preorders on \(X\).

An (acyclic) preference relation in the paper is modeled as an \emph{acyclic order} \(\%\) on \(X\), i.e., a reflexive binary relation whose strict (asymmetric) part \(\succ\) contains no directed cycle: there do not exist pairwise distinct \(z_1,\dots,z_k\in X\) with \(z_1\succ z_2\succ\cdots\succ z_k\succ z_1\).

as functions of \(n=|X|\), where \(D\) is the top-difference semimetric

location_in_paper: Section 4, page 25 (end of Section 4, after Table 1)
open_problem_quote: In passing, we note that as an immediate consequence of Theorem 4.1, we have \(\mathrm{diam}_D(A(X)) \ge \mathrm{diam}_D(P(X)) \ge n2^{n-1} + 2 - 2^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} - 2^{\lceil n/2 \rceil}.\) We do not presently know whether or not either of these inequalities hold as equalities.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The quantities are finite maxima over finite (though huge) spaces; for small n one can brute-force or use MILP/DP to compute exact diameters and look for extremal pairs, providing data to refute conjectured equalities or suggest tight formulas.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate extremal constructions (pairs of relations) can be represented explicitly (e.g., adjacency matrices/DAGs), and the fitness signal is exactly computable via the polynomial-time formula for D given in the paper; evolutionary search can explore structures to maximize D for given n and hypothesize patterns.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The top-difference semimetric \(D(\%,\mathcal D)=\sum_{S\subseteq X}|M(S,\%)\triangle M(S,\mathcal D)|\), introduced by Nishimura and Ok, measures dissimilarity of (acyclic) preference relations purely through discrepancies in induced maximal-choice sets across all menus. This choice-theoretic viewpoint connects directly to Klamler’s \(\ell_1\)-type distance on choice correspondences and to representation results of Eliaz–Ok that rationalizable choice correspondences arise as maximal-element choice from (possibly incomplete) transitive preferences. The source paper provides axiomatic characterizations of the broader \(\mu\)-top-difference family \(D_\mu\), computationally efficient formulae for evaluating \(D_\mu\), and several structural results (e.g., non-embeddability in strictly convex normed spaces).

For diameter questions, the source paper fully resolves the case of complete preferences (total preorders): it proves an exact closed form for \(\operatorname{diam}_D(P_{\mathrm{total}}(X))\) (Theorem 4.1) and notes that \(\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\ge \operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\ge \operatorname{diam}_D(P_{\mathrm{total}}(X))\), but explicitly states that it is not known whether either inequality is an equality. Related work on weighted Kendall/Kemeny-type metrics (e.g., Can; Hassanzadeh–Milenkovic) helps interpret \(D\) on linear orders and supplies tools for extremal constructions on \(L(X)\), yet does not address the broader acyclic or preorder domains. As of the provided materials (and with no forward-citation evidence supplied), the exact diameters \(\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\) and \(\operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\) remain open.

sources.title: Preference structures I: Distances between transitive preference relations
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces and studies distance functions on transitive preference relations (preorders/partial orders), extending Kemeny–Snell-type ideas beyond linear orders.
sources.connection: Provides foundational context for metric/diameter questions on spaces of (transitive) preference relations, though not for the specific top-difference semimetric \(D\).

sources.title: Preference structures II: Distances between asymmetric relations
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops distance notions for broader classes of asymmetric relations and discusses betweenness/additivity properties for preference distances.
sources.connection: Relevant as background on axiomatic/additive structure for preference distances; may inspire techniques for diameter bounds but does not treat \(D\).

sources.title: A distance measure for choice functions
sources.theorem_or_result: Defines an \(\ell_1\)-type distance on choice correspondences \(d_K(C,C')=\sum_S |C(S)\triangle C'(S)|\) and provides axiomatic characterization.
sources.connection: The top-difference semimetric \(D\) is exactly Klamler’s distance applied to choice correspondences rationalized by maximal-element choice \(M(\cdot,\%)\); informs interpretation and may guide extremal/diameter reasoning.

sources.title: Indifference or Indecisiveness? Choice Theoretic Foundations of Incomplete Preferences
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows representation of choice correspondences satisfying a relaxed WARP by maximal-element choice from a (transitive, possibly incomplete) preference relation; introduces regular preorders and behavioral indifference notions.
sources.connection: Supports the paper’s motivation that \(D\) measures discrepancies in induced (rationalizable) choice behavior; relevant for restricting to preorder domains like \(P(X)\).

sources.title: Weighted distances between preferences
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces weighted distance measures between linear orders emphasizing where rank reversals occur (weighted Kendall/Kemeny-type metrics).
sources.connection: On \(L(X)\), the top-difference metric \(D\) coincides with a particular weighted Kendall metric; these tools may help search for extremal pairs and diameter arguments, though they do not address \(A(X)\) or \(P(X)\) diameters under \(D\).

sources.title: An axiomatic approach to constructing distances for rank comparisons and aggregation
sources.theorem_or_result: Axiomatizes weighted Kendall-type distances for rank aggregation via adjacent transpositions with position-dependent weights.
sources.connection: Provides an axiomatic framework that, when specialized, recovers \(D\) on linear orders; may offer techniques for extremal distance/diameter on \(L(X)\) but not directly for \(A(X)\) or \(P(X)\).

sources.title: A Class of Dissimilarity Semimetrics for Preference Relations
sources.theorem_or_result: Defines the top-difference semimetric \(D(\%,\mathcal D)=\sum_{S\subseteq X}|M(S,\%)\triangle M(S,\mathcal D)|\); gives axiomatic characterizations; computes diameter for complete (total preorder) preferences \(P_{\mathrm{total}}(X)\); leaves \(\operatorname{diam}_D(A(X))\) and \(\operatorname{diam}_D(P(X))\) open.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open diameter questions for \(A(X)\) and \(P(X)\) under \(D\), after solving the complete-preference case (Theorem 4.1).
